title: Who Wrote the Book? Detecting and Attributing LLM Ghostwriters
abstract: In this paper, we introduce GhostWriteBench, a dataset for LLM authorship attribution. It comprises long-form texts (50K+ words per book) generated by frontier LLMs, and is designed to test generalisation across multiple out-of-distribution (OOD) dimensions, including domain and unseen LLM author. We also propose TRACE -- a novel fingerprinting method that is interpretable and lightweight -- that works for both open- and closed-source models. TRACE creates the fingerprint by capturing token-level transition patterns (e.g., word rank) estimated by another lightweight language model. Experiments on GhostWriteBench demonstrate that TRACE achieves state-of-the-art performance, remains robust in OOD settings, and works well in limited training data scenarios.

sections.heading: 1Introduction
sections.text:
Recently, a large number of AI detection datasets have been proposed (Table 1 ). However, several limitations remain. First, most datasets frame detection as binary human vs. AI classification (He et al., 2024 ; Dugan et al., 2024 ) , with only a few (La Cava and Tagarelli, 2025 ; Uchendu et al., 2021 ) supporting the authorship attribution (AA) task (i.e., multi-class classification). Second, they rarely evaluate generalisation (e.g., by including text generated by unseen generators or text from other domains). Finally, existing datasets focus on short texts, even though we are beginning to see the emergence of LLM ghostwritten books (The Authors Guild, 2024 ; Australian Society of Authors, 2025 ) . 2 2 2 Ghostwriting occurs when a writer (a.k.a ghostwriter ) produces content on someone’s behalf without authorship credit. LLM ghostwritten books are typically low-quality sham books designed to deceive customers and take sales away from legitimate books. In addition to these dataset limitations, existing attribution methods typically rely on supervised training with known authors and exhibit limited generalisation across domains and unseen models.

We approach these challenges by (i) constructing a new dataset, GhostWriteBench , for AA with multiple OOD dimensions and (ii) proposing a novel fingerprinting approach, TRACE , that is lightweight and has better generalisation capability. GhostWriteBench supports two OOD dimensions: OOD-Domain and OOD-Author, which cover genre shift and unseen LLM author evaluation, respectively. GhostWriteBench primarily focuses on long-form texts (i.e., books) generated by current frontier LLMs (on average over 50K words per document) to reflect an emerging form of AI content generation: AI ghostwriting (The Authors Guild, 2024 ; Australian Society of Authors, 2025 ) .

Next, we introduce a novel detection technique, T ransition-based R epresentation for A uthorship C lassification and E valuation ( TRACE ), for LLM attribution. TRACE first uses a (small) set of training texts generated by an LLM to create its fingerprint. At test time, to determine whether the test instance is generated by the LLM, we first create the test fingerprint and assess its similarity to the LLM’s fingerprint. To produce the fingerprint for a text (during training or inference), we examine the transitions of token-level patterns (e.g., rank of a word estimated by another lightweight language model) in the word sequence to create a compressed two-dimensional signature (see Figure 1 ). Our technique does not require white-box access and operates post hoc (and so it works for both open- and closed-source LLMs). It requires minimal training data (i.e., only a small set of LLM-generated texts), and is effective for detecting text from new domains or generated by unseen authors (LLM or human).

To summarise, our contributions are: • We introduce GhostWriteBench , a new dataset for LLM authorship attribution that supports long-form book detection and explicitly tests generalisation across two OOD dimensions. • We propose TRACE , a lightweight and interpretable fingerprinting technique for LLM authorship attribution. It achieves strong performance in-domain, OOD, and in low-resource training scenarios.

sections.heading: 2.1Authorship Attribution
sections.text: Authorship attribution (AA) is a long-standing field (Mendenhall, 1887 ; Mosteller and Wallace, 1963 ) that has grown with the rise of digital texts and advances in machine learning (Tyo et al., 2023 ; Stamatatos, 2009 ; Neal et al., 2017 ) . It is usually formulated as a multi-class classification problem, with each class representing an author. Current SOTA methods rely on neural networks, often using transformer-based backbones fine-tuned on domain-specific corpora (Tyo et al., 2023 ; Fabien et al., 2020 ; Koppel and Schler, 2004 ) . Despite their effectiveness, these approaches are resource-intensive, require careful dataset curation, generalise poorly to new domains, and struggle with long documents due to standard transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017 ) context limits (e.g., 512 tokens).

sections.heading: 2.2LLM-Generated Text
sections.text:
As LLMs are increasingly used to generate fabricated details or misinformation, provenance and veracity have become increasingly pressing (Kumarage et al., 2024 ; Huang et al., 2025 ) . Detecting LLM-generated text is non-trivial: humans often perform only slightly above chance (Clark et al., 2021 ; Dou et al., 2022 ; Uchendu et al., 2021 ) . Most prior work (Mitchell et al., 2023 ; Yang et al., 2024 ; Wu et al., 2025 ) focuses on binary (human vs. machine) classification, even though attribution to a specific model is important for accountability. Existing SOTA methods (La Cava and Tagarelli, 2025 ; Guo et al., 2024 ) largely mirror traditional AA approaches, inheriting limitations such as poor generalisation and lack of interpretability. Some works have explored cross-domain evaluation (Dugan et al., 2024 ; He et al., 2024 ; Wang et al., 2024 ) . However, they consider only a limited set of authors and exclude LLM-generated text. Most assume a closed set of authors, whereas practical attribution often occurs in open-set scenarios (Stolerman et al., 2014 ; Huang et al., 2025 ) .

LLMs have also fostered ghostwriting, producing content on someone’s behalf without authorship credit, contributing to low-quality or sham books impacting authors (The Authors Guild, 2024 ; Australian Society of Authors, 2025 ) . Motivated by these developments and methodological limitations, our work focuses on improving the generalisation of attribution techniques for LLM-generated long-form documents such as books. 3 3 3 Although ghostwriting can occur in short texts, it mainly affects long-form documents, making books a practical focus.

sections.heading: 3GhostWriteBenchDataset
sections.text:
In this paper, we focus on the task of LLM authorship attribution for long-form documents, i.e., determining which LLM generated a given document.
As shown in Table 1 , most existing datasets of LLM-generated text focus on constrained settings, i.e., (i) framing the task as binary detection, (ii) without evaluating OOD generalisation, and (iii) focusing on short texts. Motivated by these limitations, we construct a new dataset, GhostWriteBench , covering ten recent frontier LLMs (more model details in Appendix Table 9 ) and comprising books (both fiction and non-fiction) as long-form documents. 4 4 4 Our definition of long-form text is a document with more than 10 10 K words.

sections.heading: 3.1Book Generation Pipeline
sections.text:
Humans typically write book through a hierarchical process involving planning, drafting, and revision (Sun et al., 2022 ; Lee et al., 2025 ; Hayes and Flower, 2016 ) . We mimic this process by designing a book generation pipeline with incremental steps: outlining, summarisation, and long-text expansion, following prior works (Yao et al., 2019 ; Wang et al., 2025 ; Alhussain and Azmi, 2021 ) .

For each book, we first generate a detailed outline conditioned on some constraints (e.g., era and genre); this outline produces the narrative structure, plot, and characters.
And then iteratively over multiple turns, we instruct the LLM to produce the next text segment, conditioned on the outline, the previous segment, and the running summary (prompts and more details in Appendix A.1 ). We provide real, human-authored texts (sourced from Project Gutenberg 5 5 5 https://www.gutenberg.org/ . ) as few-shot reference examples to guide the LLM generation to maximise generation quality (constraints such as era and genre are therefore drawn from these Gutenberg books). This pipeline ensures that narrative coherence and discourse structure are preserved, enabling the generation of full-length books. 6 6 6 Statistics of the generation process are reported in Appendix Table 6 . Table 2 provides some data and text quality statistics for these LLM-generated books (we will return to discuss more on how we evaluate quality in Section 3.3 ).

sections.heading: 3.2OOD Dimensions
sections.text:
In addition to standard in-distribution (ID) evaluation,
the GhostWriteBench dataset examines two types of OOD generalisation: (i) OOD-Domain , to test generalisation to texts written in unseen genres; and (ii) OOD-Author , to test generalisation to texts generated by unseen LLMs.
We construct per-author domain splits, where book genres are partitioned into ID and OOD sets for each author. This creates a more challenging setting than using a single split shared across all authors, as commonly used in existing datasets (see Appendix Table 10 for splits). We use the genres defined by Momen et al. ( 2025b ) for Gutenberg books (see Table 3 for the list of genres. 7 7 7 Recall that we use Gutenberg books as few-shot reference examples for the LLM. As such, the genres for the LLM-generated books are also grounded in Gutenberg genres. In practice, a Gutenberg book often consists of multiple genres (e.g., LF (Literature & Fiction) + + ST (Science & Technology) and so many of the LLM-generated books are also multi-genre. 8 8 8 When creating the OOD-domain splits, we ensure there is no genre overlap between the ID and OOD partition; see Appendix Table 10 .

For OOD-Author, we create ten splits by holding out one LLM at a time (we have ten LLMs). That is, for a given split, the training partition contains books from nine LLMs and the test partition contains books from the held-out LLM. We report the average performance across these ten splits.

To understand the impact of training data, within a particular evaluation setting (ID, OOD-Domain, OOD-Author), GhostWriteBench also provides two training scenarios: high-resource and low-resource ; where the former has a large number (10–35) and the latter a small number of training books (3–5) text–label pairs per LLM. 9 9 9 To ensure robustness of results, we create three random splits for each subset; see Appendix Table 15 .

sections.heading: 4TRACEFingerprint
sections.text:
We next describe TRACE , a novel fingerprinting method for LLM attribution. At training time, we first construct reference fingerprints for each LLM (if a LLM has multiple books, we create one fingerprint for each book). Given a test sample, we then construct its fingerprint and determine whether it matches one of the reference fingerprints 10 10 10 Rather than performing majority voting over reference fingerprints, we retrieve the most similar reference fingerprint. .

As different LLMs may have different preferences when it comes to word selection during generation, our idea is to examine token-level transitions, such as word rank (Section 4.1 ) and entropy (Section 4.2 ), as computed by a separate, lightweight evaluator language model. Capturing word preference is not new (perplexity does that) (Wu et al., 2025 ; Huang et al., 2025 ) , but what is new here is that we want to look at how this preference transitions over time.

As illustrated in Figure 1 ,
we construct the fingerprint by modelling transitions between consecutive tokens. The resulting two-dimensional matrix produces heatmaps that reflect distinctive writing styles (see Appendix Figures 10 and 11 ). Our fingerprint TRACE can be applied to both open- and closed-source LLMs, since it does not require white-box access. It is lightweight and adaptable, and produces an interpretable 2D signature. We next discuss these two variants of TRACE : rank-based and entropy-based.

sections.heading: 4.1Rank-Based
sections.text:
Formally, given a document (or book in our case) 𝒟 \mathcal{D} represented as a token sequence ⟨ t 1 , t 2 , … , t m ⟩ \langle t_{1},t_{2},\dots,t_{m}\rangle tokenised by a tokenizer 𝒯 \mathcal{T} with vocabulary 𝒱 \mathcal{V} .
For each token t i ∈ ⟨ t 1 , t 2 , … , t m ⟩ t_{i}\in\langle t_{1},t_{2},\dots,t_{m}\rangle , we compute its rank r i r_{i} under an evaluator language model ℒ θ ^ \mathcal{L}_{\hat{\theta}} . The raw fingerprint f ^ rank ∈ ℝ | 𝒱 | × | 𝒱 | \hat{f}^{\operatorname{rank}}\in\mathbb{R}^{|\mathcal{V}|\times|\mathcal{V}|} captures the counts of ( r i − 1 , r i ) (r_{i-1},r_{i}) (Figure 1 ).
This fingerprint matrix is very large and sparse, and so we compress it by binning ranks into equal probability clusters (as detailed in Appendix Algorithm 1 ), giving the final fingerprint f rank ∈ ℝ K × K f^{\operatorname{rank}}\in\mathbb{R}^{K\times K} where K ≪ | 𝒱 | K\ll|\mathcal{V}| .

We apply this fingerprint construction process for each LLM using their training books (if an LLM has multiple books, one fingerprint is created for each book) to create reference fingerprints for each LLM. Then, for a test text, we construct a test fingerprint following the same process above. To determine which LLM generated the test text, we identify the reference fingerprint that is most similar to the test fingerprint.

To measure similarity between two fingerprints, which can be interpreted as discrete probability distributions, we use the Jensen-Shannon (JS) distance (equation given in Appendix B.1 ).

sections.heading: 4.2Entropy-Based
sections.text:
Entropy 11 11 11 Entropy and its variations are referred to by different terms in the literature, such as cross-entropy, surprisal, perplexity, and information density. has been used in NLP to capture various properties of natural language (Genzel and Charniak, 2002 ; Jaeger, 2010 ) .
Like word ranks, LLMs may exhibit unique entropy transition patterns and so constitute a potential signal for creating the fingerprint. We provide a qualitative example in Figure 2 , illustrating the generalisation of Entropy-based fingerprints.

Formally, the entropy of a token t i ∈ ⟨ t 1 , t 2 , … , t m ⟩ t_{i}\in\langle t_{1},t_{2},\dots,t_{m}\rangle as estimated by an evaluator language model ℒ θ ^ \mathcal{{L}}_{\hat{\theta}} with context t < i t_{<i} is calculated as follows: e i = − ∑ v ∈ 𝒱 ℙ ​ ( v ∣ t < i ; θ ^ ) ⋅ log ⁡ ℙ ​ ( v ∣ t < i ; θ ^ ) . e_{i}=-\sum_{v\in\mathcal{V}}\mathbb{P}(v\mid t_{<i};\hat{\theta})\cdot\log\mathbb{P}(v\mid t_{<i};\hat{\theta}). Unlike word ranks, the resulting fingerprint is continuous rather than discrete (intuitively the fingerprint is like a scatter plot). To smooth it and produce a density map (of dimension G G ),
we use kernel density estimation (detailed in Appendix Algorithm 2 ), to obtain f entropy ∈ ℝ G × G f^{\operatorname{entropy}}\in\mathbb{R}^{G\times G} where G G is the grid size of the density map.
For fingerprint matching, in addition to JS distance (as used for ranks), we experiment with another similarity metric, norm-mean, which is computed by comparing the angle between surface normals (equation given in Appendix B.1 ).

sections.heading: 6.1Main Results
sections.text:
Table 4 presents the main results on GhostWriteBench , comparing the TRACE fingerprint against baselines.
We report results separately for low-resource and high-resource authors (recall from Section 3.2 that these settings control the amount of training books available per LLM); combined results can be found in Appendix Table 11 .
Although many baselines perform well in the ID setting, they experience substantial performance degradation in our OOD setting, showing that these methods would not generalise well in a real-world application.
For standard ID evaluation, TRACE achieves the highest performance. On the more challenging scenarios: low-resource and the two out-of-distribution settings (OOD-Domain and OOD-Author), TRACE consistently outperforms both model- and metric-based baselines. A qualitative example is shown in Figure 2 . That said, for some settings with more training data (high-resource), a few baselines marginally outperform our method. For completeness, we also report results without any thresholding (only for ID and OOD-Domain) in  Appendix Table 12 , and note that TRACE still achieves performance.

sections.heading: 7Conclusion
sections.text: We propose GhostWriteBench , an authorship attribution dataset for long-form LLM texts. To test generalisation, GhostWriteBench examines two OOD settings: OOD-Domain and OOD-Author. We also introduce TRACE , a novel, interpretable fingerprint method for LLM attribution. TRACE uses token-level transition patterns, such as word ranks and entropy, to create text fingerprints. Our experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of TRACE in ID, OOD, and limited training data scenarios in GhostWriteBench . Our research provides a new benchmark that explicitly tests for generalisation and new methods that have strong generalisation capability, paving the way for future research directions in LLM text forensics.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.text:
GhostWriteBench is limited to English texts only; we plan to extend this dataset with cross-lingual OOD dimensions in future work. It would be interesting to study the effectiveness of TRACE in an OOD-language setting.
Similarly, there are other OOD dimensions, such as time, human-authors, and others, which are natural extensions of GhostWriteBench . Since TRACE is not a supervised method, we expect it to be less prone to obfuscation attacks. Still, future work needs to study the resilience of TRACE against these attacks.
Finally, adapting TRACE for fewer (<10K) tokens remains an open problem.

We also focus only on a single-authorship setup. A natural next step would be to study multi-authorship (i.e., a piece of text written by multiple authors), which is more common in long-form documents that typically involve collaborative writing. Finally, although the books generated by our pipeline are of good quality, we acknowledge that book generation and related aspects, such as creativity, self-reflection, rewriting, etc., constitute research problems in their own right and fall outside the scope of this work.

sections.heading: Ethics Statement
sections.text: GhostWriteBench contains LLM-generated books across various genres. The content of these books may include writing or plot elements that reflect undesirable values. That said, all LLMs used incorporate built-in guardrails, so the likelihood of such issues should be minimal. This is further supported by our small-scale quality annotation, where no annotators identified any issues.

sections.heading: A.1Book Generation Prompts
sections.text: As described in Section 3.1 , our book generation involves first generating the book outline (see Prompt 15 ), followed by incremental generation conditioned on the current running summary, outline, and last segment (see Prompts 16 and 17 ). For summarisation, we adapt the iterative summarisation prompts (see Prompts 13 and 14 ) from Chang et al. ( 2024 ) .

sections.heading: A.2Quantitative Analysis
sections.text: In addition to the standard language understanding metric, perplexity, we evaluate several text diversity metrics as mentioned in Table 2 . Considering we are operating in a reference-free setup, we focus on two prominent types of text diversity metrics, similarity between text pairs from the same model, and the token/type ratio (Shaib et al., 2025a ) : • Self- BLEU : Self- BLEU measures similarity between all text pairs using BLEU (Zhu et al., 2018 ) . It is a homogenisation score widely used to evaluate text diversity in writing tasks (Padmakumar and He, 2024 ) . • N N -Gram Diversity Score : This token-type ratio metric (Meister et al., 2023 ) calculates the ratio of unique n n -grams to all n n -grams in the dataset. In our analysis, we consider n n -grams of length four. • Self-repetition : Self-repetition (Salkar et al., 2022 ) measures the number of n n -grams (up to 4 4 -grams) that repeat across multiple outputs of the same model. • Compression Ratio : Defined as the ratio of the compressed file size (using gzip) of all LLM-generated texts to the original file size, this metric captures redundancy in a manner distinct from n n -gram-based measures (Shaib et al., 2025b ) . • Perplexity : We compute the average perplexity (Jurafsky and Martin, 2026 ) of model texts using the GPT-2-Large language model as a standard measure of language understanding.

sections.heading: A.3Qualitative Analysis
sections.text: Following Chhun et al. ( 2022 ); Wang et al. ( 2025 ) , we evaluate LLM-generated books across several dimensions capturing meso-, micro-, and macro-level properties. • Relevance (Rel.) : how well the text aligns with or follows the given instruction. Ensures that the generated text is appropriate and follows the given book outline. • Engagement (Eng.) : measures the degree to which the reader is engaged with the book. • Coherence (Coh.) : measures whether the story makes sense. Ensures the generated text flows logically from beginning to end, with consistent narrative elements and clear progression. • Fluency (Flu.) : measures the smoothness and natural flow of the language. • Diversity (Div.) : measures variety in linguistic expression, including rich vocabulary and varied sentence structures. • Empathy (Emp.) : measures how well the model captures the characters’ emotions, regardless of whether the reader agrees with them. • Surprise (Sur.) : measures how surprising the ending of the book is. • Complexity (Comp.) : measures how elaborate the book is.

sections.heading: A.4Cost Analysis
sections.text: Appendix Table 8 lists generation costs per LLM. In total, generating GhostWriteBench cost approximately $ 530 530 .

sections.heading: B.1Similarity Metrics
sections.text: We formulate the two similarity metrics used to compare two fingerprints, f A f_{A} and f B f_{B} , below:

figures.caption: Figure 1: Overview of TRACE fingerprint construction. We model transitions of consecutive token scores (using another language model). The process is shown for both Rank-based (§ 4.1 ) and Entropy-based (§ 4.2 ) fingerprint variants. We construct a pool of reference fingerprints from an LLM’s training texts. At inference time, attribution is a straightforward comparison between the test fingerprint and the LLM reference fingerprint.

Figure 2: An example where TRACE captures similar gpt-4.1 fingerprints across domains, demonstrating generalisation. Left: a training reference fingerprint; Middle: a test sample from seen domain ( HB ); Right: a test sample from an unseen domain ( SSP,LHH ).
Figure 3: Normalised OOD-Author confusion matrix for Entropy-based TRACE variant using norm-mean. The remaining confusion matrices for other methods can be found in Appendix Figure 9 .
Figure 4: The impact of using different fingerprint sizes for TRACE . The red dashed line denotes the selected configuration.
Figure 5: The impact of α \alpha in power law approximation for Rank-based TRACE . The red dashed line denotes the chosen α = 1.5 \alpha=1.5 .
Figure 6: The impact of the number of tokens considered per text in TRACE .
Figure 7: Evaluation of different metrics for comparing TRACE fingerprints.

Figure 10: TRACE Entropy-based reference fingerprints learned for different LLMs (in subcaption) in GhostWriteBench . We note distinct structural patterns for each LLM, suggesting they capture the writing style of the LLM.

Figure 11: TRACE Rank-based reference fingerprints (in log-scale) learned for different LLMs (in subcaption). Although not as pronounced as Entropy-based (as seen in Figure 10 ), some structural patterns are there for each LLM. Note: rank-based fingerprints have a grid structure due to the discrete nature of rank compared to continuous (scatter plot) entropy values.

Figure 13: Generate Intermediate Summary Prompt, adapted from (Chang et al., 2024 ) .
